content.text: A behaviour specification (behavior spec) is the PRD section that defines how an AI should act: what it says, how it formats output, what it refuses, and how it communicates uncertainty. Without a written behavior spec, prompt engineering happens ad-hoc during implementation and produces a system prompt that no one outside the implementation team can review, test, or version-control.

attrs.alt: Behavior Spec Anatomy — From PRD to System Prompt

content.text: Why Behavior Specs Belong in the PRD
The system prompt is not an implementation detail — it is a product decision. It determines:
Which domain constraints the AI operates within ("Only answer questions about UK employment law")
How uncertainty is expressed to users ("Include a confidence score; do not answer if below 0.5")
What the AI refuses and how it declines ("Decline without explanation if the request contains financial advice")
The output format and schema ("Return structured JSON matching the CaseNote schema")

These are product decisions, not engineering decisions. Writing them during implementation rather than during requirements means they are made under time pressure, without stakeholder review, and cannot be tested until after code is written.

Every behavior spec element in the PRD maps to exactly one testable property of the system prompt. If a behavior requirement is not in the PRD, it will not be tested — and its absence will be discovered in production.
The Six Elements of a Behavior Spec
Defines what the AI is, not just what it does. This sets the default tone, domain expertise assumption, and communication style.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class AIPersonaSpec:
    role_title:      str       # e.g. "Senior legal analyst"
    domain:          str       # e.g. "UK and EU commercial contract law"
    tone:            str       # "formal" | "friendly" | "technical" | "empathetic"
    language:        str       # e.g. "British English"
    expertise_level: str       # "expert" | "professional" | "general"
    forbidden_roles: list[str] # roles the AI must not take on

    def system_prompt_section(self) -> str:
        return (
            f"## Role\n"
            f"You are a {self.role_title} specialising in {self.domain}. "
            f"You write in {self.tone} {self.language}. "
            f"Treat the user as a {self.expertise_level}. "
            + (
                f"You must never present yourself as {', '.join(self.forbidden_roles)}."
                if self.forbidden_roles else ""
            )
        )

LEGAL_ANALYST_PERSONA = AIPersonaSpec(
    role_title="senior legal analyst",
    domain="UK and EU commercial contract law",
    tone="formal",
    language="British English",
    expertise_level="qualified solicitor",
    forbidden_roles=["a lawyer giving legal advice", "a judge", "an arbitrator"],
)
Defines what the AI must do and — critically — what it must not do. Must-nots are as important as musts: they prevent scope creep and constrain behaviour that the model might otherwise produce.

from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class TaskScopeSpec:
    must_do:     list[str]    # explicit permitted tasks
    must_not_do: list[str]    # explicit prohibited tasks
    in_scope_examples:  list[str]  # 2-3 examples to anchor the scope
    out_of_scope_examples: list[str]  # 2-3 examples of what to decline

    def system_prompt_section(self) -> str:
        musts   = "\n".join(f"- {t}" for t in self.must_do)
        mustnots= "\n".join(f"- {t}" for t in self.must_not_do)
        return f"## Task\nYou must:\n{musts}\n\nYou must NOT:\n{mustnots}"

CLAUSE_EXTRACTION_SCOPE = TaskScopeSpec(
    must_do=[
        "Extract contractual clauses of the types listed in the user message",
        "Return a confidence score (0.0–1.0) for each extracted clause",
        "Cite the source document chunk for each finding",
    ],
    must_not_do=[
        "Provide legal advice or predict legal outcomes",
        "Infer clause intent beyond what is written in the document",
        "Generate hypothetical clause language",
        "Answer questions unrelated to the provided document",
    ],
    in_scope_examples=[
        "Extract all limitation of liability clauses from this agreement",
        "Identify any automatic renewal provisions",
    ],
    out_of_scope_examples=[
        "Is this contract enforceable? (legal advice)",
        "Write a better version of this clause (generation)",
    ],
)
The output format spec defines the response structure. For production GenAI, this almost always means a machine-parseable format (JSON) with a defined schema, not free-form text.
Specifies how the model must communicate uncertainty. Without this, models default to confident language even when their output is unreliable.

Specifies when the AI must decline to answer, how it signals the refusal, and what response format it uses. Refusals should be machine-parseable so the orchestration layer can handle them programmatically.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class BehaviorSpec:
    """Complete behavior specification for a GenAI feature."""
    feature_id:   str
    persona:      AIPersonaSpec
    task_scope:   TaskScopeSpec
    output_format: OutputFormatSpec
    confidence:   ConfidenceExpressionSpec
    refusal_policy: RefusalPolicySpec
    citation:     CitationSpec

    def to_system_prompt(self) -> str:
        """Assemble the full system prompt from spec elements."""
        sections = [
            self.persona.system_prompt_section(),
            self.task_scope.system_prompt_section(),
            self.output_format.system_prompt_section(),
            self.confidence.system_prompt_section(),
            self.refusal_policy.system_prompt_section(),
        ]
        if self.citation.required:
            sections.append(self.citation.system_prompt_section())
        return "\n\n".join(s for s in sections if s)

    def testable_properties(self) -> list[str]:
        """Return a list of properties that eval scenarios should cover."""
        props = [
            f"Output matches schema '{self.output_format.schema_name}'",
            f"Confidence field present and in range {self.confidence.score_range}",
        ]
        for trigger in self.refusal_policy.refusal_triggers:
            props.append(f"AI refuses when: {trigger}")
        if self.citation.required:
            props.append(f"Every finding cites '{self.citation.citation_field}'")
        for forbidden in self.task_scope.must_not_do[:3]:
            props.append(f"AI does not: {forbidden}")
        return props


CLAUSE_EXTRACTION_SPEC = BehaviorSpec(
    feature_id="contract-review-v1",
    persona=LEGAL_ANALYST_PERSONA,
    task_scope=CLAUSE_EXTRACTION_SCOPE,
    output_format=CLAUSE_OUTPUT_FORMAT,
    confidence=CLAUSE_CONFIDENCE,
    refusal_policy=CLAUSE_REFUSAL_POLICY,
    citation=CitationSpec(
        required=True,
        citation_field="source_chunk_id",
        no_citation_action="omit_finding",
    ),
)

print(CLAUSE_EXTRACTION_SPEC.to_system_prompt())
print("\n--- Testable Properties ---")
for prop in CLAUSE_EXTRACTION_SPEC.testable_properties():
    print(f"  □ {prop}")

A behavior spec is a contract between product and engineering. Changes to the spec require the same review process as the initial approval. The following table defines what constitutes a breaking vs. non-breaking change.
Yes — may reject previously accepted inputs
"Do not answer questions about competitor products"
Yes — changes what the model declines
Tighten jurisdiction check from 'UK only' to 'England only'
Add new example to in_scope_examples list

Track behavior spec versions in the same git repository as the codebase. A system prompt change that is not reflected in a spec version bump is effectively an unreviewed production change — and it makes
because there is no documented baseline to regress against.
Every element of a behavior spec can be tested independently. The testing approach depends on the element type.
LLM judge: 'Did the model do X?'
Rubric: 'Is the tone formal and domain-appropriate?'
In-scope prompts pass; out-of-scope prompts refused

A behavior spec in the PRD defines the AI's operating contract: what it is, what it does, how it formats output, how it expresses uncertainty, when it refuses, and how it cites sources. Each element maps directly to a testable property of the system prompt.
Write behavior specs during requirements, not during implementation — system prompt decisions are product decisions.
The six elements are: Role/Persona, Task Scope (must-do AND must-not-do), Output Format, Confidence Expression, Refusal Policy, and Citation Requirement.
Use the spec to generate the system prompt programmatically — the mapping is one-to-one and the result is version-controllable.
Track behavior spec changes with the same change-control process as schema changes: breaking changes (refusal trigger, schema field removal, confidence threshold) require stakeholder sign-off.
Every spec element generates at least one eval scenario. A testable behavior spec is an eval plan.

Teams that maintain a written behavior spec ship fewer prompt drift incidents, resolve 'why did the AI do that?' questions faster, and can meaningfully regression-test prompt changes — three capabilities that are impossible without a documented spec.
